architecture_fit.note: Randomly-initialized cross-attention model; latest run lift is ~1.5e-7, indistinguishable from uniform — it does not track LCS alignment at all.

baseline_comparison.note: Only the benchmark's built-in uniform reference exists; no real strawman-vs-working contrast is constructed, and all four heads are identical random projections.

faithfulness.note: No ablation or causal check; the model uses no actual mechanism (untrained random weights), so there is nothing faithful to verify.

operating_range.note: Evaluated only on the single canonical condition; no input-scale sweep or breaking-point analysis.

hardcoded_weights_bonus.note: README claims a hand-built circuit (exact/shifted/random/uniform heads) but main.py implements none of it — it is random init, so the claimed hand-set weights do not exist.

visual_judgement.note: Bar charts of mass/lift per head load fine but show flat ~baseline bars; the heatmap is an empty 'not saved' placeholder.

visualisation_rationale.note: README justifies the bar plot but describes heads that do not match the code, so the rationale is disconnected from what is actually computed.

notes: The README advertises a hand-built four-head LCS circuit, but main.py actually runs a randomly-initialized cross-attention model with no hand-set weights, so the claim is unsupported by the code. The latest benchmark confirms the model performs at chance (lift ~1.5e-7, robustness ~0), failing the goal's core question of whether a head tracks LCS alignment. With no faithfulness check, no operating-range sweep, and a visualisation describing heads that do not exist in the code, this attempt fails across the rubric.
